---
title: "Assessment & Grading"
page-layout: full
---

## How Grading Works — Specifications Grading

**Every assignment is graded pass / not-yet, never with partial points.** Each one comes
with a short, explicit list of standards ("specifications") you can see before you start. If
your work meets all of them, it passes. If it doesn't yet, you get specific feedback and
**revise and resubmit** — "not-yet" is a step in the process, not a penalty. You are never
averaged down for an early attempt. Your final letter grade reflects **how many deliverables
you complete to standard**, so the path to the grade you want is simply to meet the standards
on more of the work. Nothing here is curved, weighted, or scored on a percentage.

The full policy — bundle definitions, the revise budget, and the complete ladder — is
published on the [homeworks grading page]({{< var homeworks_url >}}/grading.html).

---

## The 8 Deliverables

The graded work is **8 deliverables**, each pass / not-yet:

- **U0 — Tooling & Reproducibility** — the onboarding **completion floor**. It doesn't by
  itself raise your letter, but every grade above E requires it.
- **Six core units** — **U1, U2a, U2b, U3, U4, U5** — the rungs of the ladder.
- **Capstone** — independent synthesis: choose a model, fit it, quantify uncertainty by a
  valid Bayesian method, and report results + ethics.

Unit-by-unit descriptions, objectives, and what to submit are on the
[homeworks site]({{< var homeworks_url >}}) (also linked from [Homeworks](homeworks.qmd) in
the navbar). Assignment materials are distributed via **GitHub Classroom** — submissions are
pushed to your private repo, visible only to you and Greg.

### What it takes to pass a unit

A unit passes only when **all three bundles** are met:

1. **Correctness** — autograded method-validity / outcome specs (the core gateway).
2. **Interpretation (CERL)** — Claim, Evidence, Reasoning, Limits — human-confirmed.
3. **Process** — reproducibility and the submission contract.

Miss a bundle and the unit is **not-yet**: you revise *that bundle* and resubmit, not the
whole unit.

---

## The Letter Ladder

Letters are straight **A B C D E** (no plus/minus). **U0 must be passed in every case
above E.**

| Letter | Requirement |
|--------|-------------|
| **A** | all 6 core units **and** the Capstone |
| **B** | all 6 core units, **or** 5 core + Capstone |
| **C** | 5 core units, **or** 4 core + Capstone |
| **D** | 4 core units |
| **E** | 3 or fewer core units, **or** U0 not passed |

The Capstone **gates the A** and is otherwise worth one core unit of lift at the B/C
boundaries — capped, so it can never substitute for full core coverage on the way to an A.

---

## Revise & Resubmit Budget

- **Correctness (autograded): unlimited resubmissions** until the unit's deadline — the
  autograder re-runs on every push at no human cost, so keep pushing until it passes.
- **Interpretation + Process (human-confirmed): one** revise-and-resubmit per unit, **plus a
  course-wide pool of 3 tokens** for a second human-bundle revision on the units you find
  hardest.

**Dates** (deadlines and post-feedback revision windows): TODO — calendar pending.

---

## Other Course Components

*The components below are part of the course design but carry **no percentage weight** —
nothing in this course is point-scored. How each integrates with the specifications ladder
is being finalized (TODO).*

### Projects

Two integrative projects replace traditional midterm and final exams. Each synthesizes
multiple weeks of the course arc.

**Project 1: "One Problem, Three Lenses"** — *Due Week 5 (TODO — calendar TBD)*

Use a single regression problem with a clinical dataset. Solve it three ways:

- **Least Squares**: classical normal equations and interpretation
- **Maximum Likelihood Estimation**: Gaussian model, negative log-likelihood
- **Bayesian**: posterior inference and credible intervals

Synthesis question: How do the three approaches diverge and converge? What assumptions differ?

**Project 2: "From Inference to Discovery"** — *Due Week 7 (TODO — calendar TBD)*

Same dataset, now tasked with discovering structure:

- **Approximate Inference**: variational inference or sampling
- **Discriminative Classification**: logistic regression or SVM
- **Unsupervised Learning**: k-means clustering + PCA for dimensionality reduction

Synthesis question: What story does the data tell when you stop presuming its structure?

**Submission**: GitHub Classroom. See [GitHub Classroom]({{< var classroom_url >}}) for the
invite link.

### Checkpoints

Three retakeable in-class assessments (Weeks 2, 4, 6):

- **Checkpoint 1 (Week 2)**: Linear regression, bias–variance, regularization
- **Checkpoint 2 (Week 4)**: Classification, likelihood, Bayesian inference
- **Checkpoint 3 (Week 6)**: Unsupervised learning, dimensionality reduction

Each checkpoint is offered twice (original + retake within 1 week); you keep your best
result. **Dates**: scheduled during class time. TODO — calendar pending.

### Peer-Engagement Loops

Six weekly loops (Weeks 1–6): complete the interactive tool, generate your token, post to
D2L, review peers, integrate feedback. See [Activities](activities.qmd) for loop details.

### Prerequisite Quiz

A brief diagnostic (administered Week 1) on linear algebra and probability fundamentals.
Most students pass; it exists to surface gaps early, not to gatekeep.
